title: Nonmanufacturing Business Outlook Survey (NBOS) - March 2026 Report
summary: The Philadelphia Fed's Nonmanufacturing Business Outlook Survey for March 2026 shows nonmanufacturing activity in the region declined, with the firm-level general activity index falling 14 points to -8.3 (first negative reading since June) and regional activity index dropping to -23.9 (lowest since May). Employment indexes turned negative for both full-time (-11.3) and part-time (-7.2) positions, while price indexes suggest continued but moderating price increases.
key_points: Firm-level general activity diffusion index fell to -8.3, first negative reading since June 2025; 19% reported increases, 27% decreases, 54% no change
Full-time employment index fell 22 points to -11.3 (lowest since August 2024); 14% reported decreases, only 2% reported increases
Prices paid index ticked down to 39.1 but still indicates widespread input price increases; prices received index fell to 9.3 (lowest since June)
77% of firms cite energy markets as at least a slight constraint on operations, up sharply from 38% last quarter
71.9% of firms expect uncertainty impacts to worsen over next three months; 76.5% expect energy market impacts to worsen
Future firm-level activity index fell 19 points to 12.5 (lowest since December), suggesting less widespread growth expectations ahead
why_it_matters: This regional service sector survey provides a real-time pulse on business conditions in the Philadelphia Fed's district, which is used by Fed officials for policy assessment. The sharp deterioration in employment indexes and forward-looking indicators suggests service sector weakness could be spreading, while the surge in energy market concerns (cited by 77% vs 38% prior) signals input cost pressures are weighing on business sentiment. The turning negative of employment indexes may signal cooling labor market conditions in the service sector.
macro_context: The survey indicates contraction in regional nonmanufacturing activity with employment turning negative, suggesting the service sector may be losing momentum. The sharp increase in energy market constraints (77% vs 38%) and widespread expectations for worsening uncertainty and energy pressures over the next three months point to downside risks for the economic outlook. These readings are consistent with slowing service sector activity that could influence Fed rate path considerations.

issues_found: PUBLISHED_AT was empty in source metadata but release date (March 24, 2026) was correctly extracted from article body

notes: This is a regional business survey from the Philadelphia Fed district, covering nonmanufacturing (service) sector firms. Survey responses collected March 9-19, released March 24, 2026. Not market structure related. Confidence slightly lowered due to regional (not national) scope—may not be representative of national nonmanufacturing activity. Special questions show notable deterioration in energy market concerns and broad uncertainty about the outlook.
